title: Number Partitioning
summary: Split a list of numbers into two groups whose sums are as close as possible.
problem_statement: I have a set of 8 positive numbers: [3, 1, 1, 2, 2, 1, 5, 9]. Split them into two groups so the absolute difference between the two group sums is as small as possible. Tell me which numbers go in each group.
real_world_example: Dividing a list of tasks between two workers so each finishes at roughly the same time, or splitting items between two delivery trucks so the loads balance.

expected_pattern.variables: s_i in {-1, +1} — which group number i belongs to
expected_pattern.constraints: none — the squared imbalance is the entire objective

expected_solution_summary: A perfect split exists: {9, 1, 2} (sum 12) vs {5, 3, 1, 2, 1} (sum 12). Difference = 0.
learning_notes: Number partitioning is the textbook 'pure Ising' problem: spin variables, quadratic objective, no constraints. The squared imbalance expands to a quadratic form over the spins. The same shape appears in graph max-cut and clustering.

learning_objectives: Distinguish spin (±1) variables from binary (0/1) and know when to use each
Recognize that a squared-difference objective expands into a QUBO
Notice that 'no explicit constraints' doesn't mean 'trivial' — the objective itself encodes the structure
